ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for preserved sea coconut (attap palm fruit). Closest defensible anchor is palm fruit food product (FOODON_03315873). Sea coconut in the Singaporean context refers to the preserved endosperm of the nipa or attap palm, not the Lodoicea maldivica botanical sea coconut.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers coconut and palm fruits as a category. No specific code exists for preserved attap palm endosperm. A00LC used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for preserved sea coconut / attap palm fruit endosperm.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of preserved sea coconut in a Singaporean dessert context is approximately 80 g, equivalent to a small bowl or a standard dessert component in ice kachang or chendol.

nutrient_source.title: Sea Coconut — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sea Coconut (cr_id F04010039). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for preserved sea coconut (attap palm endosperm) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with canned fruit in syrup (GI ~60–70, University of Sydney GI Database). The high sugar content (14.1 g/100g) and low fibre (0.6 g/100g) support a medium-to-high GI estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (65 × 17.0 g available carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 11.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (14.1 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium (8 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 50 g or less; avoid pairing with sweetened dessert soups or syrups, as the combined sugar load can drive insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevate blood pressure in hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 14.1 g/100g with only 0.6 g dietary fibre per 100 g results in rapid glucose absorption and a medium-to-high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~11).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict to a small portion (30–50 g) and pair with a protein or fat source to blunt postprandial glucose response; avoid sea coconut served in sweetened syrup preparations, which substantially increase the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.0 g/100g confirmed zero by formulation) and very low sodium (8 mg/100g); however, high added sugar content (14.1 g/100g) warrants portion control to avoid excess free-sugar intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea coconut is low in fat and sodium and poses minimal direct cardiovascular risk, but the high sugar content should be moderated to stay within AHA free-sugar guidelines (≤25 g/day for women, ≤36 g/day for men); consume in small portions as an occasional dessert component.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present (no omega-3, polyphenols, or adequate fibre); high sugar content in syrup preparations is pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sea coconut provides no positive anti-inflammatory benefit and is frequently served in high-sugar syrup desserts that are pro-inflammatory; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer anti-inflammatory fruits such as berries or citrus and limit sea coconut to occasional small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea coconut (preserved) is the sugar-syrup preserved endosperm of the attap or nipa palm (Nypa fruticans), widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a dessert ingredient. It is characteristically translucent, jelly-like, and mildly sweet, with a soft, slightly chewy texture. In Singapore, it is a classic component of ice kachang, chendol, and various cold dessert soups. The fruit is harvested young, when the endosperm is still semi-liquid to gelatinous, then preserved in sugar syrup for commercial use. Per 100 g, preserved sea coconut provides 86 kcal, 21.3 g carbohydrate (of which 14.1 g is sugar), 0.3 g protein, 0.0 g fat (confirmed zero by product formulation), and only 0.6 g dietary fibre. Sodium is very low at 8 mg/100g. The high sugar and low fibre profile means it has a medium estimated glycaemic index (~65) and a glycaemic load of approximately 11 per 80 g serving. Clinically, it is suitable for most healthy adults in moderate portions but requires caution for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high free-sugar content and negligible fibre buffering. It is plant-based, vegan, halal, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: Is sea coconut the same as attap chee?
How many calories in sea coconut?
What is sea coconut made of?
Is sea coconut high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04010039) with confidence 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate from canned fruit in syrup (University of Sydney GI Database) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero: preserved sea coconut endosperm contains no fat by product formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. saturated_fat_g and cholesterol_mg are also confirmed zero for the same reason. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo for this entry. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sea coconut as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~65, medium) based on canned fruit in syrup from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for preserved attap palm endosperm exists in any approved source. Note on naming: in Singapore, 'sea coconut' (海椰子) refers to the preserved attap/nipa palm endosperm used in desserts, not the botanical Lodoicea maldivica (double coconut) of the Seychelles. The food_subgroup has been updated from 'Fresh fruit' to 'Preserved fruit' to accurately reflect the processed, sugar-syrup preserved nature of this product.
